threats.title: transferFrom Fee Deduction Bypasses ERC20 Allowance, Draining Sender Beyond Approval
threats.hypothesis_description: _payTxFee deducts transaction fees directly from the sender's balance via _update() without consuming any allowance. Then super.transferFrom() only checks and consumes allowance for the transfer amount. Total tokens deducted from sender = amount + fees, but allowance only covers amount. Any approved spender can force the sender to lose more tokens than approved.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the core ERC20 allowance invariant. At 100% fee rate (allowed by setTxFeeRate), an approval of N tokens lets the spender extract 2N tokens from the sender. Even at normal fee rates, every transferFrom extracts extra tokens beyond what the sender authorized.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _payTxFee calls _update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees) which bypasses allowance; then super.transferFrom only deducts 'amount' from allowance

preconditions: Sender has approved spender for some amount
_txfeeRate > 0 (non-zero fee rate configured by ADMIN)
_feesFaucet != address(0)
Sender has sufficient balance for amount + fees

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: _update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees) deducts fees without allowance check; super.transferFrom only consumes 'amount' from allowance
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Total tokens removed from sender (amount + txFees) exceeds the allowance approved by sender
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to include fees in allowance consumption or check total deduction against allowance

analysis_questions: Can a spender repeatedly call transferFrom to drain sender beyond initial approval?
What is the maximum extractable amount at various fee rates?
Does this affect DeFi integrations that rely on standard ERC20 allowance semantics?
validation_requirements: Trace exact balance and allowance changes through transferFrom with non-zero fee
Calculate maximum extraction: at 100% fee, approval of X allows extracting 2X from sender
Test with standard DeFi patterns (approve + transferFrom)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Sender approves spender for 1000 EURF. Fee rate is 1% (100 bps). Spender calls transferFrom(sender, recipient, 1000). _payTxFee deducts 10 EURF fee from sender (no allowance check). super.transferFrom transfers 1000 EURF from sender and consumes 1000 from allowance. Sender loses 1010 EURF total but only approved 1000. At 100% fee rate, spender extracts 2000 EURF with a 1000 approval.
prerequisites: Non-zero fee rate must be set
Any user with any ERC20 approval can exploit

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Every transferFrom extracts extra tokens beyond approval. At maximum fee rate (100%), impact doubles the approved amount. Breaks all DeFi integrations relying on allowance semantics.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who have granted any ERC20 approvals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix; extracted tokens are in feesFaucet

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for total deduction vs allowance comparison in fee-bearing transferFrom

threats.title: Forwarder Accepts Arbitrary Domain Separator Enabling Cross-Contract/Cross-Chain Signature Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: The Forwarder.execute() accepts domainSeparator as an external parameter and passes it directly to _verifySig() without validating it against the Forwarder's own contract address or chain. Any valid EIP-712 signature from the user on any contract with the same ForwardRequest struct layout can be replayed on this Forwarder by passing the original contract's domain separator.
threats.investigation_rationale: EIP-712 domain separators exist specifically to prevent cross-contract replay. By accepting arbitrary domain separators, this protection is entirely bypassed. The project targets Ethereum and Polygon, making cross-chain replay a concrete threat for multi-chain users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: domainSeparator parameter used directly in digest computation without validation against contract's own domain; Forwarder has no EIP-712 domain separator to compare against

preconditions: User has signed a ForwardRequest on another Forwarder instance or chain with matching struct format
Target EURF address matches req.to on the target Forwarder
User nonce on target Forwarder matches the signed nonce (e.g., both 0 for new users)
User has sufficient balance on target chain for transfer + fees

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bytes32 digest = keccak256(abi.encodePacked("\x19\x01", domainSeparator, ...)) uses caller-supplied domainSeparator
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No comparison of domainSeparator against a contract-derived value; Forwarder does not inherit EIP712 and has no own domain separator
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing: require(domainSeparator == _computeDomainSeparator()) or inheriting EIP-712 for domain binding

analysis_questions: Does the project deploy Forwarders at the same address on Ethereum and Polygon?
Can signatures from OpenZeppelin's MinimalForwarder (same struct) be replayed here?
What is the nonce state of users on secondary Forwarder deployments?
validation_requirements: Verify Forwarder has no internal domain separator computation
Test cross-chain replay scenario with matching EURF addresses
Test replay from a different Forwarder instance on same chain

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User signs ForwardRequest on Polygon Forwarder (nonce=0, to=EURF_polygon, transfer 1000 EURF to recipient). Same user has never used Ethereum Forwarder (nonce=0). If EURF addresses match across chains, attacker submits the same signature to Ethereum Forwarder with Polygon's domain separator. Signature verifies because domainSeparator is unchecked. Transfer of 1000 EURF executes on Ethereum without user intent.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized transfers of user tokens on secondary chain/Forwarder. Full balance at risk per replayed transaction.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Tokens transferred to attacker-controlled recipient are unrecoverable without forceTransfer

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for cross-contract or cross-chain signature replay with different domain separators

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction, after obtaining signature from another context
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Replay detectable by monitoring nonce consumption on multiple Forwarders

threats.title: Revoked Controller Can Re-Enable Safety Switch Despite Role Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: The safetySwitch re-enable path checks _operatingController == _msgSender() but does NOT verify the caller still holds the CONTROLLER role. A controller who activated the switch and was subsequently removed from the CONTROLLER role retains the ability to re-enable operations, undermining the emergency halt mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a window where a compromised controller that was deactivated can still undo the emergency shutdown, allowing malicious minting/burning to resume. The only other re-enable path requires DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Re-enable branch: if (!hasRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, _msgSender()) && _operatingController != _msgSender()) revert; Missing: && hasRole(CONTROLLER, _msgSender()) check

preconditions: Controller C activated safetySwitch (set _operating=false, _operatingController=C)
Owner removed C from CONTROLLER role via removeController(C)
C still has access to their private key

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Revoked controller address is stored in _operatingController and can still pass the re-enable check
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing hasRole(CONTROLLER, _msgSender()) requirement in re-enable path

analysis_questions: Can the owner preempt this by calling safetySwitch themselves to re-enable and clear _operatingController?
Is there a way to clear _operatingController without re-enabling operations?
Can the compromised controller re-enable and then immediately trigger malicious minting?
validation_requirements: Verify removeController does not clear _operatingController
Test: controller activates switch → owner removes controller → removed controller calls safetySwitch
Confirm DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE can also re-enable (as alternative remediation path)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Controller C is compromised and activates safetySwitch to halt operations as cover. Owner removes C's CONTROLLER role. C then calls safetySwitch() again - the check `_operatingController != _msgSender()` passes (C == C). Operations resume. C (with colluding minter) can now mint/burn despite role revocation.
prerequisites: Controller must have activated the safety switch
Controller role must have been revoked afterward

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Emergency halt can be circumvented, allowing unauthorized minting/burning to resume
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders if combined with compromised minter
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can pause the contract as alternative emergency mechanism

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for removed controller attempting to re-enable safety switch

threats.title: MASTER_MINTER With MINTER_ROLE Gets Unexpectedly Constrained by Minter Allowance
threats.hypothesis_description: In mint(), the allowance check applies to any caller with MINTER_ROLE regardless of also holding MASTER_MINTER. If an address holds both roles, the MINTER_ROLE allowance check is enforced, unexpectedly limiting a MASTER_MINTER who should have unlimited minting capability. The second if-block checks hasRole(MINTER_ROLE) without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a logic defect where the code contradicts the tiered minting design. MASTER_MINTER is intended to have unlimited minting authority, but holding both roles triggers allowance enforcement. This could block legitimate minting operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender())) applies allowance check without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders; should be: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()) && !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()))

preconditions: An address holds both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE simultaneously
The MINTER_ROLE allowance for that address is below the desired mint amount

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender())) { ... allowance check ... } applies regardless of MASTER_MINTER status
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: MASTER_MINTER's unlimited minting is suppressed if they also have MINTER_ROLE
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()) condition in the allowance check block

analysis_questions: Can addMinter be called with the MASTER_MINTER address as target?
Is there any scenario where MASTER_MINTER automatically receives MINTER_ROLE?
Does this affect the setMasterMinter flow?
validation_requirements: Test: grant both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE to same address, attempt mint exceeding allowance
Verify addMinter does not prevent adding an existing MASTER_MINTER
Check if setMasterMinter grants MINTER_ROLE alongside MASTER_MINTER

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: MASTER_MINTER address is also granted MINTER_ROLE via addMinter(masterMinterAddr, 1000). Master minter attempts mint(recipient, 5000). The access check passes (has MASTER_MINTER). But allowance check triggers (has MINTER_ROLE) and reverts because 5000 > 1000 allowance. Master minter is unexpectedly limited.
prerequisites: Same address must hold both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational disruption of minting capabilities for dual-role holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol operations team managing token supply
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Removable by revoking MINTER_ROLE from the MASTER_MINTER address

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for dual-role (MASTER_MINTER + MINTER_ROLE) minting behavior

threats.title: Phantom Fee Requirement Blocks Transfers When feesFaucet Is address(0) But Fee Rate Is Non-Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: _payTxFee checks balanceOf(from) < txFees + txAmount and reverts if insufficient, but only transfers fees when _feesFaucet != address(0). When _feesFaucet is address(0) and _txfeeRate > 0, the balance check requires fee headroom that is never actually deducted, creating a phantom balance requirement that blocks transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script does not call setFeeFaucet. If ADMIN sets a fee rate but the feesFaucet is never set (remains address(0)), all transfers require balance >= amount + fees but no fees are collected. Users with exact balance for a transfer are denied service.
deployment_assumptions: _txfeeRate set to non-zero while _feesFaucet remains address(0)
threats.config_evidence: deployToken.js does not call setFeeFaucet() or setTxFeeRate(); both remain at default (0/address(0)). Risk materializes if ADMIN sets fee rate without first setting feesFaucet.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (balanceOf(from) < txFees + txAmount) revert; followed by if (_feesFaucet != address(0)) _update(...); Balance check always applies but fee deduction is conditional

preconditions: _txfeeRate > 0 (set by ADMIN)
_feesFaucet == address(0) (not configured or cleared)
User has balance >= amount but < amount + calculateTxFee(amount)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Balance sufficiency check includes fees that are never deducted when feesFaucet is zero
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Users locked out of transfers due to phantom fee requirement
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No early return or adjusted check when _feesFaucet == address(0)

analysis_questions: Is there any deployment step that atomically sets both feesFaucet and txFeeRate?
Can setFeeFaucet be called with address(0) to clear it while fee rate remains active?
How many users would be affected by the phantom balance requirement?
validation_requirements: Test transfer with feesFaucet=address(0) and txFeeRate>0
Verify _update is skipped but balance check still enforced
Confirm no fee is collected but transfer is blocked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: ADMIN sets txFeeRate to 100 (1%) via setTxFeeRate but forgets to set feesFaucet. User with exactly 1000 EURF tries to transfer 1000 EURF. _payTxFee requires balance >= 1010 (1000 + 10 fee). Transfer reverts. The 10 EURF fee would never be deducted since feesFaucet is address(0), but the check still blocks the transfer.
prerequisites: Admin misconfiguration: fee rate without feesFaucet

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS on transfers for users with insufficient phantom headroom; tokens effectively frozen
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting transfers when misconfigured
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can fix by setting feesFaucet or clearing fee rate

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for fee behavior when feesFaucet is address(0)

threats.title: Shared Nonce Pool Between permit() and transferWithAuthorization() Enables Cross-Function Invalidation
threats.hypothesis_description: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() share the same _nonces mapping via _useNonce(). Executing one function increments the nonce, invalidating any pending signed message for the other function at the same nonce value. An entity holding a valid signed TWA can use it to invalidate a victim's permit (or vice versa).
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard EIP-2612 and EIP-3009 implementations use separate nonce mechanisms. The shared nonce creates a cross-function invalidation vector that can grief users whose signed authorizations become invalid when the nonce advances unexpectedly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Both permit and transferWithAuthorization call _useNonce(owner/holder) which increments the same _nonces[owner] counter

preconditions: User has signed both a permit and a transferWithAuthorization with the same nonce value
Attacker or third party holds one of the signed messages
One message is executed before the other

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Single _nonces mapping used by both permit and transferWithAuthorization
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Executing permit(nonce=N) invalidates a pending transferWithAuthorization(nonce=N) and vice versa
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No separate nonce space per function type; no EIP-3009 random nonce

analysis_questions: Can an attacker who legitimately received a transferWithAuthorization use it to grief a permit?
Are there DeFi integrations that batch permit + transferFrom that would be affected?
Does the Forwarder path interact with the same nonce space?
validation_requirements: Test: sign permit at nonce N, sign TWA at nonce N, execute TWA, verify permit fails
Verify Forwarder nonces are separate from token _nonces
Assess impact on batched meta-transaction workflows

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User signs permit(nonce=5) for DApp A and transferWithAuthorization(nonce=5) for DApp B. DApp B executes TWA first, incrementing nonce to 6. DApp A submits permit(nonce=5) which reverts due to nonce mismatch. User's approval for DApp A silently fails.
prerequisites: User has signed messages at same nonce for different function types

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service on pending signed authorizations; potential loss of time-sensitive approvals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: User must re-sign with new nonce

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for cross-function nonce invalidation between permit and TWA

threats.title: Forwarder verify() and execute() Have Inconsistent Validation Allowing Misleading Off-Chain Checks
threats.hypothesis_description: Forwarder.verify() does NOT check req.to == _eurfAddress, while execute() does. A request that passes verify() off-chain may fail execute() on-chain. This inconsistency can mislead relayers into submitting transactions that will revert, wasting gas without receiving the gasless base fee.
threats.investigation_rationale: Relayers use verify() to pre-validate requests before spending gas on execute(). The missing req.to check in verify() creates a false positive that leads to wasted gas when execute() reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: verify() checks nonce, sig, and selector but NOT req.to == _eurfAddress; execute() checks all four

preconditions: A ForwardRequest has req.to != _eurfAddress but valid signature and correct selector
Relayer uses verify() to pre-check before calling execute()

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: Forwarder.sol lines 60-72 (verify) missing req.to check present at line 87 (execute)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Asymmetric validation between verify() and execute()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: verify() returns success for requests that execute() will revert on

analysis_questions: Do production relayers rely on verify() before calling execute()?
Can this be used to grief relayers into wasting significant gas?
Are there other validation differences between verify() and execute()?
validation_requirements: Test: construct request with wrong req.to, call verify (passes), call execute (reverts)
Quantify gas wasted per griefing attempt
Check if relayer fee (gaslessBasefee) is lost on revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker signs a valid ForwardRequest with req.to set to a non-EURF address. Relayer calls verify() which passes all checks. Relayer calls execute() which reverts at require(req.to == _eurfAddress). Relayer loses gas cost. The gaslessBasefee is never paid since execute() reverts entirely.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Relayer gas griefing; no token loss but operational cost to relayers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Relayers can add off-chain req.to check

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing verify() and execute() validation paths

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via repeated revert patterns from same address

threats.title: mint() and burn() Bypass Pause and Blacklist Checks Allowing Supply Manipulation During Emergency
threats.hypothesis_description: mint() and burn() only check _operating flag but NOT paused() state or blacklist status. When the contract is paused for an emergency, minters can still create and destroy tokens. Additionally, tokens can be minted to blacklisted/sanctioned addresses, violating compliance controls.
threats.investigation_rationale: For a regulated EUR stablecoin, the ability to mint to sanctioned addresses or manipulate supply during an emergency pause represents a critical compliance gap. The burn() NatSpec explicitly claims 'Validates that sender is not blacklisted' but no such check exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mint() checks hasRole + _operating + allowance but NOT paused() or isBlacklisted(to); burn() checks hasRole + _operating but NOT paused() or isBlacklisted(_msgSender())

preconditions: Minter or MASTER_MINTER role is compromised or colluding
Contract is paused and/or target address is blacklisted

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No call to adminSanity, no paused() check, no isBlacklisted() check in either function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Supply manipulation continues during emergency pause; blacklisted addresses can receive minted tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing: whenNotPaused modifier and blacklist validation on to/sender

analysis_questions: Can a minter mint while contract is paused and then transfer once unpaused?
Does minting to a blacklisted address create compliance liability?
Is the safety switch (_operating=false) sufficient protection?
validation_requirements: Test: pause contract, call mint - verify it succeeds
Test: blacklist address X, call mint(X, amount) - verify it succeeds
Test: burn from blacklisted minter - verify it succeeds

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin pauses the contract during an emergency. A compromised minter calls mint(attacker, 1000000) which succeeds because mint doesn't check paused(). When contract is unpaused, attacker has inflated tokens ready to sell.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized token minting during emergency; compliance violation by minting to sanctioned addresses
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders affected by supply inflation; regulatory exposure for protocol
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can forceTransfer back or burn excess tokens if minter cooperates

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for mint/burn during paused state or to blacklisted addresses

threats.title: setOwner May Revoke Wrong Address If Multiple OWNER Role Members Exist
threats.hypothesis_description: setOwner performs grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) then revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)). It assumes index 0 is always the current owner. If the OWNER role has multiple members (via direct grantRole call), setOwner revokes index 0 which may not be the caller, leaving additional hidden owners with full upgrade and admin authority.
threats.investigation_rationale: The single_owner invariant is critical for this UUPS-upgradeable stablecoin. Hidden owners can perform upgrades, role changes, and other critical operations. The EnumerableSet swap-on-removal behavior makes the ordering unpredictable after modifications.

proof_of_signal.pattern: grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) adds to set; revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)) assumes index 0 is the old owner; with >1 members, index 0 may be wrong after EnumerableSet swap

preconditions: Current OWNER has directly called grantRole(OWNER, anotherAddress) at some point
Multiple addresses hold OWNER role when setOwner is called

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Non-atomic role transfer relying on EnumerableSet index 0 assumption
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After grantRole adds newOwner, index 0 may still be old owner or may be a different member depending on set ordering
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1 before operation; no explicit revocation of caller

analysis_questions: Does the inherited grantRole prevent granting OWNER to additional addresses outside setOwner?
What is the exact EnumerableSet ordering after add + remove operations?
Can this be triggered by the deployment script's setOwner call?
validation_requirements: Test: grantRole(OWNER, B) directly, then setOwner(C), verify only C has OWNER
Trace EnumerableSet add/remove ordering with 3 members
Verify deployment: initialize grants OWNER to deployer, setOwner transfers to INITIAL_OWNER_ADDRESS

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner A directly calls grantRole(OWNER, B). Set=[A,B]. A calls setOwner(C). grantRole adds C: set=[A,B,C]. revokeRole(getRoleMember(0)) revokes A. Set=[C,B] (swap). B retains OWNER privileges invisibly. B can upgrade the contract implementation.
prerequisites: OWNER must first create multi-owner state via direct grantRole

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Hidden owner retains full protocol control including UUPS upgrade authority
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders if hidden owner upgrades to malicious implementation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Detectable via getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) check; fixable by revoking extra members

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for setOwner with >1 existing OWNER members

threats.title: payGaslessBasefee Bypasses Pause and Blacklist Checks Allowing Token Movement During Emergency
threats.hypothesis_description: payGaslessBasefee() only checks isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender) before calling _update(payer, paymaster, _gaslessBasefee). It does not check paused() state or blacklist status of payer or paymaster. A malicious or compromised trusted forwarder can extract tokens from any address (including blacklisted) even when the contract is paused.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the Forwarder's execute() calls transfer() which checks pause/blacklist, payGaslessBasefee is a separate external function. If the trusted forwarder is compromised or if ADMIN sets a malicious address as trusted forwarder, this function becomes an unrestricted token extraction mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only checks isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender) and balance; no paused(), isBlacklisted(payer), or isBlacklisted(paymaster) checks before _update

preconditions: Trusted forwarder is compromised or maliciously set
Contract is paused or payer/paymaster is blacklisted
_gaslessBasefee > 0

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Blacklisted addresses can send/receive tokens; paused state has no effect
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing adminSanity(payer, paymaster) or equivalent pause/blacklist checks

analysis_questions: Can the trusted forwarder call payGaslessBasefee in a loop to drain a user's entire balance?
Is _gaslessBasefee bounded by any maximum?
What is the rate of extraction (per transaction)?
validation_requirements: Test: pause contract, call payGaslessBasefee from trusted forwarder - verify it succeeds
Test: blacklist payer, call payGaslessBasefee - verify it succeeds
Calculate maximum extraction rate

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: ADMIN is compromised and sets malicious contract as trusted forwarder. Malicious forwarder repeatedly calls payGaslessBasefee(victim, attacker) to drain victim's balance by _gaslessBasefee per call, bypassing all pause and blacklist protections.
prerequisites: Compromised ADMIN sets malicious trusted forwarder

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Token drainage from any address at rate of _gaslessBasefee per call, bypassing all compliance controls
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any token holder targeted by the malicious forwarder
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can revoke ADMIN or change trusted forwarder

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for payGaslessBasefee behavior during pause or with blacklisted addresses

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Low gas per call, repeatable in loop

threats.title: Blacklisted ADMIN Can Bypass Sender Blacklist and Pause via adminSanity Exemption
threats.hypothesis_description: adminSanity() exempts ADMIN role holders from both pause check and sender blacklist check. A blacklisted admin can call transfer() and transferFrom() even when the contract is paused and they are blacklisted as sender. This means blacklisting an admin address does not prevent them from moving their own tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an ADMIN account is compromised, the owner might blacklist the admin and pause the contract as emergency measures. However, the compromised admin can still transfer tokens out because adminSanity exempts admins from both checks. The owner must revoke ADMIN role first, creating a race condition.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!hasRole(ADMIN, _msgSender())) { if (paused()) revert; if (isBlacklisted(from)) revert; } — ADMIN skips both pause AND sender blacklist

preconditions: ADMIN account is compromised
Owner blacklists the ADMIN address
ADMIN role is not yet revoked

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ADMIN check bypasses entire block containing both pause and sender blacklist checks
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Blacklisting an admin has no effect on their ability to transfer as sender
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Blacklist should apply to all addresses regardless of role for outbound transfers

analysis_questions: Can the owner revoke ADMIN role before the admin can transfer tokens out?
Does the admin exemption also apply to transferWithAuthorization?
Is this intentional design for compliance force-transfer workflows?
validation_requirements: Test: blacklist admin, pause contract, admin calls transfer - verify it succeeds
Verify admin exemption applies to all three transfer functions
Check if recipient blacklist still applies (line 66)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: ADMIN account compromised. Owner blacklists admin and pauses contract. Compromised admin calls transfer(attacker_wallet, full_balance) - adminSanity exempts them from both pause and sender blacklist. Tokens moved before owner can revoke ADMIN role.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Compromised admin can move tokens despite blacklist/pause, limited to admin's own balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner must revoke ADMIN role as first action, not blacklist

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for blacklisted admin attempting standard transfers

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction race with owner's role revocation
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Transfer events from blacklisted address visible

threats.title: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init Has Public Visibility Instead of Internal
threats.hypothesis_description: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init is declared as 'public onlyInitializing' instead of 'internal onlyInitializing'. While protected by the onlyInitializing modifier, during the initialization phase it could be called by external actors to re-execute ERC20 init, AccessControlEnumerable init, and reset MASTER_MINTER to address(0) and _operating to true.
threats.investigation_rationale: All OpenZeppelin initializer functions follow the pattern of 'internal onlyInitializing'. The public visibility is a code defect that expands the attack surface during initialization. Combined with a complex initialization sequence, this could allow state manipulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public onlyInitializing — should be 'internal onlyInitializing' per OZ convention; re-executes __ERC20_init, __AccessControlEnumerable_init, and resets MASTER_MINTER to address(0)

preconditions: Contract is in the initializing state (during deployment)
External caller can interact during the same initialization transaction or nested initializing context

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: External call during initialization could reset MASTER_MINTER to address(0) and re-run ERC20 init
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Function should be internal to prevent external invocation

analysis_questions: Can this function be called externally during the initialize() transaction?
Does onlyInitializing fully prevent re-initialization in all scenarios?
What state changes would occur if called during a reinitializer context?
validation_requirements: Verify onlyInitializing prevents external calls outside initializer context
Test calling __ERC20ControlerMinter_init externally during and after initialization
Check if reinitializerV2 context could allow this to be called

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During a future upgrade with reinitializer(2), if __ERC20ControlerMinter_init is called externally, it re-executes __ERC20_init (potentially resetting name/symbol), re-grants MASTER_MINTER to address(0), and resets _operating to true. This could disrupt the role hierarchy and operating state.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: MASTER_MINTER reset to address(0) could break minter management; _operating reset could undo safety switch
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol administration and all token holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can re-set MASTER_MINTER via setMasterMinter

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for external call to __ERC20ControlerMinter_init

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Role change events would be emitted
